description: MITRE ATT&CK rules for database forensics analysis. These rules analyze feature vectors from MySQL binlog, PgAudit, and Percona audit pipelines covering SQL operations, privilege changes, schema modifications, authentication events, and composite activity scores.

rules.description: Detects mass data deletion activity by identifying an abnormally high number of DELETE operations combined with elevated destructive operation counts. Mass deletion is a strong indicator of data destruction attacks, ransomware preparation, or insider threat activity aimed at causing operational disruption or covering tracks after data exfiltration.
rules.recommendation: Immediately halt destructive operations on affected databases. Assess the scope of data loss by comparing current state against the most recent verified backup. Preserve transaction logs and binlogs before any recovery attempt. Initiate point-in-time recovery from the last known-good backup. Investigate the user accounts and source IPs responsible for the DELETE operations. Check for data exfiltration preceding the mass deletion.

rules.description: Identifies suspicious mass UPDATE operations that may indicate data manipulation, corruption, or tampering. An unusually high volume of UPDATEs can signal an attacker modifying records to cover tracks, corrupt business data, or alter audit trails. This pattern is also seen in automated attack scripts that modify sensitive fields at scale.
rules.recommendation: Identify the specific tables and columns targeted by mass UPDATE operations. Compare affected records against backup data to determine the nature and extent of modifications. Preserve the current database state and transaction logs for forensic analysis. Review the SQL statements in audit logs to identify whether updates were targeted or indiscriminate. Check for automated scripts or compromised application accounts executing the updates.

rules.description: Detects potential privilege escalation by identifying excessive GRANT operations combined with elevated privilege change events. This pattern is consistent with an attacker or compromised account systematically elevating privileges across multiple database users, potentially establishing persistent backdoor access or preparing for further exploitation.
rules.recommendation: Audit all GRANT statements to identify which privileges were assigned and to which accounts. Revoke any unauthorized privilege escalations immediately. Review the privilege hierarchy to identify over-privileged accounts. Check for newly created superuser or DBA-level accounts. Cross-reference privilege changes with authentication logs to identify the source of the escalation. Implement least-privilege access policies.

rules.description: Identifies suspicious mass creation of database user accounts. Creating a large number of users in a short period is a strong indicator of backdoor establishment, where an attacker creates multiple accounts to maintain persistent access even if some accounts are discovered and removed.
rules.recommendation: List all newly created database accounts and verify each against authorized provisioning requests. Disable or remove any unauthorized accounts immediately. Audit the privileges assigned to each new account. Check if any new accounts have superuser or elevated privileges. Review the source IP and session information for the account creation statements. Implement approval workflows for account creation.

rules.description: Detects an abnormally high volume of GRANT operations indicating systematic privilege assignment. This pattern may indicate an attacker distributing elevated privileges across multiple accounts to ensure persistent access, or a compromised admin account being used to weaken the security posture of the database environment.
rules.recommendation: Audit all GRANT statements to determine which permissions were assigned and to which accounts. Compare the current privilege matrix against the authorized baseline. Revoke any unauthorized or excessive privileges. Investigate the sessions and users responsible for the GRANT operations. Implement database activity monitoring to alert on future privilege changes. Review and enforce role-based access controls.

rules.description: Identifies mass privilege revocation activity which may indicate an attacker locking out legitimate administrators by removing their access, or covering tracks by removing previously granted backdoor privileges. Mass REVOKE operations can be used as part of a denial-of-service attack against database administrators.
rules.recommendation: Determine which accounts had privileges revoked and whether these were authorized changes. Restore privileges for legitimate administrators who were locked out. Investigate the timing and source of REVOKE operations. Check if the revocations were preceded by privilege escalation activity that is now being cleaned up. Verify that no backdoor accounts retain elevated privileges while legitimate accounts were demoted.

rules.description: Detects mass DROP TABLE operations that indicate deliberate data destruction or database sabotage. Dropping multiple tables is a destructive action typically associated with ransomware attacks, disgruntled insiders, or post-exploitation cleanup where the attacker destroys evidence after data exfiltration.
rules.recommendation: Immediately suspend database access for the responsible account. Assess data loss by listing dropped tables from the information_schema or system catalog backups. Initiate recovery from the most recent verified backup. Preserve all transaction logs and audit records for forensic analysis. Investigate the user session and source IP responsible for the DROP operations. Check for any preceding data exfiltration activity.

rules.description: Detects DROP DATABASE operations which represent the most severe form of database destruction. Any DROP DATABASE event is critical as it destroys an entire database including all tables, views, procedures, and associated data. This action is almost never performed in production environments and strongly indicates malicious intent.
rules.recommendation: This is a critical severity event requiring immediate incident response. Disable the responsible database account. Verify backup integrity and initiate full database recovery. Preserve all audit logs, binary logs, and WAL files for forensic chain of custody. Investigate how the attacker obtained DROP DATABASE privileges. Review network logs for the source of the destructive session. Engage incident response team for full investigation.

rules.description: Identifies unusual database creation activity. Creating multiple databases may indicate an attacker establishing staging areas for data exfiltration, deploying webshells via database-backed applications, or setting up command-and-control infrastructure using database features such as SQL Server linked servers or PostgreSQL foreign data wrappers.
rules.recommendation: List all newly created databases and inspect their contents. Check for databases with names mimicking system databases or using random/encoded names. Verify each database creation against change management records. Inspect the contents of new databases for staged data, webshell code, or exfiltration staging. Remove any unauthorized databases after preserving their contents for evidence.

rules.description: Detects excessive schema modification events including CREATE, ALTER, and DROP operations on tables and other database objects. Mass schema changes can indicate an attacker restructuring the database to facilitate data exfiltration, installing triggers or stored procedures for backdoor access, or modifying table structures to bypass application-level security controls.
rules.recommendation: Compare the current database schema against the baseline schema to identify all changes. Check for newly created triggers, stored procedures, or functions that could serve as backdoors. Inspect ALTER TABLE operations for modifications to sensitive columns or constraints. Verify all DDL changes against change management approvals. Look for schema changes that weaken security constraints or data validation rules.

rules.description: Identifies an abnormally high transaction volume that deviates from normal operational patterns. Excessive transactions may indicate automated attack scripts, data scraping operations, or denial-of-service attacks targeting the database. This pattern can also indicate a compromised application executing unauthorized operations at scale.
rules.recommendation: Analyze the transaction distribution over time to identify burst patterns. Correlate high-volume transaction periods with application deployment schedules and known batch operations. Identify the specific user accounts and source applications responsible for the highest transaction volumes. Check for automated scripts or bot activity. Review transaction contents for patterns consistent with data harvesting or systematic data modification.

rules.description: Detects an abnormally high volume of INSERT operations that may indicate data staging for exfiltration, database flooding attacks, or the insertion of malicious data. Large INSERT bursts can also indicate an attacker importing tools, scripts, or exfiltrated data into the database for later retrieval through a different channel.
rules.recommendation: Identify the target tables receiving the INSERT operations and inspect the nature of inserted data. Check for binary data, encoded payloads, or data patterns consistent with exfiltration staging. Analyze the timing and rate of inserts to distinguish between legitimate ETL operations and malicious activity. Verify against scheduled data import operations. Monitor outbound connections from the database server for data exfiltration attempts.

rules.description: Identifies excessive authentication events and authentication changes that may indicate brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, or an attacker testing stolen credentials against the database. A high volume of authentication activity combined with account changes suggests active exploitation of the authentication system.
rules.recommendation: Analyze authentication event logs for patterns of failed login attempts followed by successful logins. Identify the source IPs and user accounts involved. Implement account lockout policies and rate limiting. Check for password spraying patterns across multiple accounts. Verify that all successful authentications following multiple failures are legitimate. Reset passwords for accounts showing suspicious authentication patterns.

rules.description: Detects a high volume of error events combined with a reduced success rate, indicating brute-force password attacks or credential stuffing campaigns against the database. A sudden drop in success rate with elevated error counts is a classic indicator of automated credential testing tools targeting database authentication.
rules.recommendation: Identify the source IPs generating failed login attempts and block them at the firewall. Determine which user accounts were targeted. Check if any accounts were successfully compromised after the brute-force attempt. Implement progressive delay or lockout after failed authentication attempts. Review database server firewall rules to restrict authentication sources. Enable connection-level rate limiting.

rules.description: Detects excessive database configuration changes that may indicate an attacker modifying security settings, disabling audit logging, weakening authentication requirements, or altering server parameters to facilitate further exploitation. Configuration tampering is a common defense evasion technique used to reduce the effectiveness of security monitoring.
rules.recommendation: Review all configuration changes to identify modifications to security-relevant parameters such as authentication settings, audit configurations, and access controls. Compare current configuration against the baseline to detect unauthorized changes. Restore any tampered security configurations. Check for disabled audit logging or modified log destinations. Investigate the sessions and accounts responsible for configuration changes.

rules.description: Detects potential audit log tampering by identifying excessive configuration changes combined with unknown or unclassified events. This pattern suggests an attacker is modifying the audit system to suppress future logging, alter existing log entries, or generate noise to obscure malicious activity within the audit trail.
rules.mitre_technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging
rules.recommendation: Immediately verify the integrity of the audit logging system. Compare audit log files against secure backup copies. Check for gaps in the audit trail timeline. Verify that all audit configuration parameters match the security baseline. Investigate the unknown events to determine if they represent log corruption or injection. Enable immutable audit logging by sending logs to a separate, write-once system. Preserve all current audit logs as forensic evidence.
